mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Goldenseal contains isoquinoline alkaloids such as berberine and hydrastine; NCCIH notes low berberine absorption from oral goldenseal may limit applicability of berberine-only studies. A controlled clinical study (PMC) indicates goldenseal can precipitate drug interactions via inhibition pathways (e.g., CYP3A/CYP2D6 and transport processes), but mechanisms vary by drug and product.
activeCompounds: Berberine and (+)-hydrastine are two well-characterized alkaloids frequently used as reference constituents for goldenseal chemistry discussions.
safetyNotes: NCCIH: short-term doses ~3 g/day without serious harm in small studies; long-term safety uncertain; products may be adulterated/mislabeled. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and do not give to infants (newborn jaundice/kernicterus risk from berberine).
description: A North American plant with bright yellow rhizome; native to northeastern U.S. and southeastern Canada; wild populations have been overharvested and are limited.
dosage: About 3 g/day has been used in small research studies for short periods (longer-term safety uncertain).
interactions: NCCIH reports a study in healthy adults where metformin levels decreased ~25% with goldenseal extract + metformin, potentially affecting glucose control; NCCIH also flags broad interaction potential. The controlled trial article (PMC) specifically frames goldenseal as capable of clinical drug interactions via CYP inhibition effects.
preparation: Oral supplements (commonly rhizome/root-derived); also used as mouthwash/eyewash and topically in some contexts, though NCCIH emphasizes limited human evidence for efficacy.
region: Native range is SE Canada to N. Central & E. U.S.A.
sideEffects: potential liver toxicity with long use
summary: Goldenseal is promoted for colds/URIs and other uses, but NCCIH states there is not enough evidence to determine usefulness and notes no rigorous human studies evaluating effects on health conditions. Short-term dosing around 3 g/day has appeared tolerated in small studies, but longer-term safety is uncertain. Key caution: possible medication-interaction effects (including reduced metformin levels in a clinical study) and pregnancy/breastfeeding/infant avoidance due to berberine risk in newborns.
